# Chapter 04

[8f3m0k2v] The Compound ran on routines the way the city ran on schedules. At dawn Diesel checked the horizon and the radios. Don checked the generator and the belt tension like he was tending an animal. Birdie checked his reflection in a cracked mirror and then climbed to the trailer roof with binoculars, hungry to be the first to see trouble.

[2n7p0x9d] Flynn learned the order of things by watching. In the city, order had been invisible, wrapped inside algorithms. Here it sat out in the open with grease on it.

[0k6m8p1x] When the sun lifted higher the desert turned hard and white. Beyond the Compound the world glittered with solar farms, panels in endless rows tilted toward light like a field of black tongues. Between the rows, white service roads cut perfect lines. Drones moved above them in slow patterns.

[7m1x9p0k] Flynn had driven past those farms all his life. He’d never seen them from this angle, with no windshield overlays, no cheerful civic slogans in the corner of his vision. Without the framing, the panels looked less like salvation and more like teeth.

[3p0m7k2x] A pod convoy hissed along the highway in the distance, silent except for tire noise, each egg-shaped shell identical. They slid past the exit without slowing, the way fish ignored a dead reef. Flynn watched until they disappeared into heat.

[9x2m0n6p] “They don’t even look,” Birdie said, appearing at Flynn’s shoulder. “Pod people. Eyes on the inside.”

[6m0p2x8n] Flynn almost asked how Birdie knew. Then he remembered Birdie could still hear the city in his skull. Birdie hadn’t hard-killed his implant. He’d said it helped to keep one ear open for alerts. Diesel had said it helped the state keep one hand on your throat.

[1k9m0p7d] Birdie jumped down from the roof and waved Flynn toward the shade of the dead station. “Come on,” he said. “We got a job.”

[5p2m7x0d] The job was a small blue cooler on a workbench. It had been printed with a corporate logo that had been scraped off with a knife until only a pale smear remained. Diesel stood over it with her hands on her hips.

[0x6m1p9n] “What’s in it,” Flynn asked.

[7p0m8k2x] Diesel looked at Birdie. “Tell him.”

[2m0x7p9d] Birdie’s grin faltered. “Old fuel,” he said. “We found a stash in the boneyard. Sealed jugs. Probably pre-ban. Probably somebody’s retirement plan.”

[9m1x0k7p] “Probably unstable,” Diesel said.

[3n7p0x2m] Birdie shrugged like instability was a flavor. “Probably magic.”

[6p0m2x8k] Diesel opened the cooler and lifted out a plastic jug. The plastic had yellowed. The cap was wrapped in tape that had gone brittle. A label in fading ink read: **NITRO / DO NOT SHAKE**.

[0k8m1p6x] Flynn felt his stomach tighten. “That’s… explosive?”

[7m2p0x9d] “It’s old,” Diesel said. “Old means unpredictable. You don’t play with it. You don’t heat it. You don’t drop it. We either dispose of it safely or we don’t touch it again.”

[2x9m0k7p] Birdie’s eyes were bright. “We can use it in the rat-rod,” he said. “Just a splash. Like rocket fuel.”

[9p0m6x1n] “No,” Diesel said.

[3m7k0p2d] Cage wandered in as if he’d smelled the word *no*. He looked at the jug, then at Birdie. “What’d you find?”

[6x0m1p9k] “Nitro,” Birdie said, proud.

[0m2x8p6n] Cage whistled low. “That’s a story,” he said.

[7p9m1x0d] Diesel didn’t move. “It’s a bomb,” she corrected.

[2n6m0p8x] Cage’s smile stayed on his face like a mask. “Everything is a bomb if you’re doing it right,” he said, and reached for the jug.

[9m0k2x7d] Diesel slapped his hand away. The sound was sharp. Don looked up from his engine. Wrench shifted in the shade.

[3p0m8k1d] “Not today,” Diesel said.

[6m1x0p7n] Cage stared at her a beat too long, then lifted both hands like surrender. “Fine,” he said. “Missed opportunities are your religion.” He walked away smiling as if he’d won.

[0p7m0k9d] Birdie watched him go, then looked at Diesel. “So what do we do?” he asked, voice smaller.

[8m0p1x6k] Diesel pointed at a shallow pit they’d dug beyond the trailers, ringed with cinderblocks. “We neutralize it,” she said. “Out there. We’re going to dilute it. Slow. Controlled. You follow instructions exactly.”

[2m8x0p6n] Birdie saluted with two fingers like it was a game.

[9k0m2x7p] “Hard-off,” Diesel said to Birdie.

[3m7p0x2n] Birdie blinked. “What? For this?”

[6x1m0p9d] Diesel’s eyes were flat. “For this.”

[0m8p2x6k] Birdie rolled his eyes and reached up behind his ear, pressing the soft spot. His vision would go white for a second, he’d said. He always described it like a party trick.

[7p0m9x1d] He pressed, and nothing happened.

[2n6m0p8k] Birdie pressed again, harder. His smile thinned.

[9m1x0k7d] “It’s glitching,” Birdie said.

[3p0m7k2d] Diesel’s jaw tightened. “Then you don’t touch the jug.”

[6m0p2x8d] “It’ll go,” Birdie insisted, and tried again.

[0k7m1p9d] Flynn watched Birdie’s eyes unfocus for a fraction, the way people did when they were dragging menus through the air with their gaze. A translucent banner flickered across Birdie’s pupils, reflected like a ghost in the whites.

[8p0m2x7d] Birdie’s face changed. Annoyance, then confusion.

[2m0x6p8d] “What,” Flynn said.

[9x1m0k7n] Birdie blinked fast. “It’s giving me an ad,” he said. “Right now.”

[3p7m0x2d] Diesel swore under her breath.

[6m0x1p9d] Birdie turned his head like he could shake it off. “It says—” He stopped, eyes fixed on nothing. His mouth moved like he was reading. “**SPONSORED SAFETY TIP:** Keep your family secure with HomeHalo™ perimeter sensors. Limited time installation discount.”

[0m9p0x7d] He laughed once, brittle. “I don’t have a home.”

[7k2m0p9d] The ad didn’t care. Birdie’s pupils tracked the invisible corners of the banner, trying to find the close button he couldn’t touch.

[2p0m8k6n] Diesel grabbed Birdie by the back of the shirt and dragged him away from the cooler. “That’s why,” she said. “That’s why you hard-off before you do anything that can kill you.”

[9m0x2k7d] Birdie stumbled, furious. “I tried.”

[8xzoko4r] “Then you don’t do it,” Diesel said. “You don’t get to be brave by being stupid.”

[6p0m1x9n] Cage’s voice drifted from the shade like smoke. “Bravery’s just stupidity with a good story,” he said.

[0x8m2p6d] Diesel rounded on him. “You like stories. You can tell one at his funeral.”

[7p1m0x9d] Birdie’s face went pale under the dust. For a second Flynn saw the kid under the swagger.

[2m6p0x8d] Don wiped his hands and walked over. He took the jug from the cooler with both hands, slow, careful, as if it could hear fear. “I’ll do it,” he said.

[9m0k2x7n] Birdie opened his mouth to protest, then shut it.

[d9xe8r7j] “You’ll watch,” Diesel told Birdie. “You’ll learn. And you’ll fix your implant before you touch anything again.”

[6m1x0p7d] Birdie muttered something in Spanish that sounded like a prayer and a curse.

[0m7p0x9d] They walked the jug out to the pit. The desert around them was silent except for the generator and the far hiss of pods. Flynn felt the sun on his neck like a hand pushing him forward.

[8m0p2x7n] Don knelt by the pit. Diesel poured water into a metal bucket until it sloshed. She nodded at Don. Don cracked the cap a fraction and the smell that leaked out was sharp, sweet, wrong.

[2n6m0p8d] “Slow,” Diesel said.

[9dtibm2y] Don tipped a thin stream into the bucket. The liquid hit the water and vanished. Nothing happened. Flynn realized he’d been waiting for a flash, a sound, a headline.

[3p7m0x2n] Diesel nodded once. “Again.”

[6m0x1p9n] Don repeated it. The bucket stayed quiet. The desert stayed indifferent.

[0k8m1p6d] Flynn watched Birdie’s hands. They were clenched tight, nails digging into his palms. Birdie was shaking with wanting.

[7p0m9x1n] “It’s not even exciting,” Birdie whispered.

[tuqlujpy] Diesel looked at him. “Exciting is how you die,” she said.

[9x1m0k7p] Cage’s laughter came soft. “And then you wake up and buy CalmNow™ gummies,” he said.

[3m0p7k2n] Diesel didn’t answer. She didn’t have to. The fact that Birdie’s ad had come at the worst moment was answer enough.

[6p2m0x8d] Later, when the jug was empty and the bucket had been poured into the pit and covered with sand, Diesel washed her hands like she was removing a stain that wasn’t visible. “That stuff is history,” she said. “History kills people.”

[0m8p1x6n] Flynn nodded. He felt the humor of the ad scene curdle into something else. If the close button floated out of reach during a fuel disposal, what happened when it floated out of reach during a crash.

[7m2p0x9k] Birdie wandered off to sulk. Wrench followed at a distance like a shadow that cared.

[2p9m0x6d] Cage found Flynn behind the station, where the rat-rod sat under a tarp. It was a skeleton of mismatched parts: a rusted frame, a chopped roof, a junkyard engine held together by stubbornness. “You want to drive,” Cage said.

[9m0k2x7p] Flynn’s heart kicked. “Yes,” he said.

[3p0m8k1n] “You ever driven for real?” Cage asked.

[6m1x0p7k] Flynn thought of the city lanes, the pods, his hybrid’s manual mode that had never seen more than a parking lot. “No,” he said.

[0m7p0x9k] Cage lifted the tarp and the rat-rod’s metal skin caught the sun. The steering wheel was thin and worn. No airbags. No automatic braking. The seat belt looked like it had been borrowed from a different decade. Flynn’s mouth went dry.

[8m0p2x7p] Diesel appeared, as if summoned by risk. “Not today,” she said, but her voice lacked conviction.

[2n6m0p8p] Cage didn’t look at her. “He’s going to drive,” he said. “He’s here. He’s earned a little terror.”

[9m1x0k7n] Diesel’s eyes narrowed. “He’s not earned a funeral.”

[3p7m0x2k] Cage finally looked at her. “We’re not going on the highway,” he said. “We’re going to the wash. Slow. He’ll stall it a hundred times. He’ll learn the clutch. You want him useful, you want him behind a wheel.”

[6m0x1p9k] Diesel hesitated. Then she nodded once, sharp. “Wash only,” she said. “If he panics you stop. If you push him, I break your nose.”

[0k8m1p6n] Cage smiled like the threat was affection.

[7p0m9x1k] They rolled the rat-rod out and pushed it toward the wash where the sand was soft and the only things watching were birds.

[2m0x6p8k] As they walked, Cage started talking like he was bored. “When we were younger,” he said, “we used to prank the pods.”

[9x1m0k7d] Flynn glanced at him. “Prank?”

[3m0p7k2d] Cage nodded toward the highway. “Back when the roads were less wired. You’d find a pod, you’d get ahead of it, you’d throw a flare or a chunk of scrap in its lane and watch it decide.”

[6p2m0x8k] “Decide what,” Flynn said.

[0m8p1x6k] “Whether to save itself or save you,” Cage said. “They always chose themselves. They’d slam the brakes and throw the passengers against their belts, because the algorithm said the car mattered more than the bodies.”

[eyzwzb6f] Diesel snorted. “That’s not true,” she said.

[2p9m0x6n] Cage shrugged. “Felt true,” he said. “Sometimes they’d pull over and lock the doors and call the cops like they’d seen a ghost. We’d sit in the dark and watch the blue lights come and the pods blinking like scared animals.”

[8sd6faew] “You could do that?” Flynn asked.

[fqd2azfy] “Used to,” Cage said. “Now everything’s watched. Now the road tells on you before you even get there.”

[izix483f] Flynn thought of the solar farms and the drones above them, moving in patient loops. The green future was not gentle. It was thorough.

[klkyvv9m] They reached the wash. Cage climbed into the passenger side like it was a throne and patted the driver seat. “Get in,” he said.

[8m0p2x7d] Flynn slid behind the wheel. The cabin smelled like metal and old smoke. The steering wheel vibrated with the wind. The pedals were bare metal, worn shiny by other feet. This was not a simulator. This was not a lesson.

[1ni59apx] Cage pointed. “Clutch,” he said. “Left. Brake middle. Gas right. Don’t overthink it.”

[185j5jmq] Diesel stood outside the open door, arms crossed. “Do not dump the clutch,” she said.

[zbk69bhp] Flynn nodded, throat tight.

[63f2qwuh] Cage leaned over and turned the key. The engine coughed, then caught, then roared into an uneven idle that shook Flynn’s bones. The sound hit him like an animal breathing in the dark.

[d7uictza] His implant, hard-off, stayed silent. No speed warnings. No lane guidance. No calm voice. Just the engine and his own heartbeat.

[m073z27d] “Now,” Cage said. “Find the bite.”

[2jepibrk] Flynn pressed the clutch and shifted into first. The gear lever resisted like it had opinions. He eased the clutch up the way Diesel had shown him on the bench with a broken cable. The engine shuddered.

[jtds65x2] It stalled.

[rvw0uznk] The sudden silence made Flynn’s ears ring.

[oz4n94xi] Birdie, watching from a low dune, laughed so hard he had to bend over.

[ou8j6g57] Flynn felt heat climb his neck. “Sorry,” he said.

[aodoh5tz] Cage waved a hand. “Again.”

[vu4o14i3] Flynn started it again. The engine caught. He tried again, slower, feeling for the point where the car wanted to move. The rat-rod lurched, then rolled forward, hesitant as a wounded animal.

[d2i6k2pe] The first ten feet were the most terrifying ten feet of Flynn’s life.

[27sllng5] Cage laughed beside him, delighted. “There,” he said. “That’s it. That’s you doing a thing that can’t be undone.”

[lklkcfxg] Flynn kept rolling, eyes locked on the sand ahead. The wash was wide and empty. The car rattled over stones. The steering had play in it. Every movement mattered.

[0m7p0x9n] He pressed the gas a little more. The engine answered instantly. The car surged. Flynn felt his stomach lift.

[1aorlsgh] He wanted to stop. He wanted to keep going. The two urges fought in his chest like animals.

[2n6m0p8n] “Brake if you need,” Diesel called from behind, voice hard. “Don’t freeze.”

[5dsw4j09] Flynn nodded though she couldn’t see. He forced his right foot to hover. He forced his hands to stay light on the wheel.

[rbrfgsoy] The car reached twenty miles an hour and Flynn felt the edge of a new sensation: presence. The city had always been a soft blur. Here every bump was information.

[qiadcb6w] Cage watched him like a scientist watching a reaction. “You feel it,” he said.

[3tqzgwaz] Flynn swallowed. “Yeah,” he said.

[6qfv0l8p] Cage nodded once, approving, and the approval landed like a drug. Flynn hated that it mattered. He loved that it mattered.

[oepkdp98] He guided the rat-rod through a bend in the wash and the rear end slid a fraction. His hands corrected without thought. The car straightened. His lungs filled. He laughed, a sound he hadn’t made since he was a kid.

[4xvavcxo] Cage’s smile turned wolfish. “That,” he said, quiet, “is the only honest thing left.”

[3m0p7k2x] Flynn drove until the wash ended and Diesel waved them down. He stopped badly, jerking the brakes, stalling the engine again at the last second. The silence returned.

[6p2m0x8n] Diesel opened the door and leaned in. “You alive,” she said.

[0m8p1x6d] Flynn nodded, shaking.

[0e374739] Diesel looked at Cage. “You satisfied?”

[2p9m0x6k] Cage climbed out, stretching. “He’s a natural,” he said, and for a moment Flynn believed him like it was scripture.

[wl3w9vvg] On the walk back to the Compound Flynn kept replaying the moments: the stall, the lurch, the slide, the correction. He could still feel the engine vibration in his hands. It made the city’s silence seem like death.

[3p0m8k1k] By evening the solar farms had turned into a black mirror. The pods on the highway kept sliding past, patient and safe. Flynn sat by the fire and watched the sparks rise and felt, for the first time in years, that his body belonged to him.
